text:
The optical properties of biomass-burning organic carbon (OC) undergo significant transformations during atmospheric aging, yet the governing factors remain poorly understood. This study investigates the evolution of light absorption for smoldering wood smoke aerosol photochemically aged in a $104\,\text{m}^3$ outdoor dual chamber under natural sunlight. Using a UV-visible spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere, we observed a dynamic diurnal pattern in the mass absorption cross section (MAC) of OC. In most cases, the integrated MAC ($280–600\,\text{nm}$) increased by 11–54% during the morning hours, likely due to the formation of secondary chromophores from phenolic precursors, before declining by 19–68% in the afternoon. 

High relative humidity (RH) was found to accelerate this bleaching process, whereas elevated $\text{NO}_x$ levels slightly sustained the aerosol color, suggesting that $\text{NO}_x$ promotes the formation of resilient nitrogen-containing chromophores. Chemical characterization revealed a rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity and levoglucosan concentrations over time, indicating the degradation of primary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and sugar markers. Furthermore, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy demonstrated that while the aerosol became more oxygenated, its hygroscopicity decreased with aging. These findings, corroborated by ambient data collected during a major Florida wildfire, suggest that biomass-burning OC overall becomes less light-absorbing after approximately 8–9 hours of solar exposure. This dynamic evolution of "brown carbon" absorptivity should be accounted for in radiative forcing models to more accurately assess the climate impact of biomass combustion.
